author: @Shattered.01
text:
It's Forged in Fire. One of the reasons Will Willis encountered conflict after the first season was his questioning the validity of the tests,and the results. Also considering Doug is a knife expert not a martial arts sword expert and Dave Baker was a prop maker when he started. I've seen plenty of better crafted weapons lose in the final rounds with out failing, decisions I could not understand.
Yes, the table was cut because that's what they do in WWE wrestling, use destructable props.

replies.author: @John_Redcorn_
replies.text: Doug IS an edged-weapon martial arts expert. He has trained lots of different militaries in such. I forget the name but its a style from the Philippines

replies.author: @Shattered.01
replies.text: @John_Redcorn_ edged weapon as in knife. He is proficient in Kali, a martial art focused on knife and stick fighting.

replies.author: @josephleisses3997
replies.text:
WWE uses wood ladders painted to look aluminum. They're lighter, which is easier to carry and safer to break (making the performers look stronger. You can tell they're wood with some of the breaks the ladders have, albeit you have to look closely.
It's a show, after all. Earlier ladders were metal, but it got changed to what it currently is, because Vince McMahon was in a ladder match and learned how much it sucked to carry around and how much it hurt to get hit with even "safely".

text: There is a great interview with the original host, somewhere in YouTube-land.  They got rid of him when he started having an issue with the judges. He was even questioning their bona fides, in that one of the “experts” was just a prop maker for Hollywood.

replies.author: @nephatrine
replies.text: Actually WWE tables are usually not precut/gimmicked. They just buy cheap particle board tables in bulk and those break easily to begin with. Also WWE doesn't use wooden ladders. These are clearly supposed to be based on wrestling props, but aren't _actual_ wrestling props.

author: @merakimelodies8931
text: Saw it in my feed and clicked immediately! Love y’all’s videos. 😊 Hey, I have a historical weapon-suggestion y’all may want to investigate in the future: I believe it’s called a hungamunga, and it’s of African origins: pretty-nifty thrown-weapon. Also check out a dagger I believe is called a Haladi: it’s a double-edged knife from India. I thought y’all might enjoy researching them, since they are so unique and odd, and have so much history to them! KoA had a Haladi for sale, once: they may still have one.

author: @winoodlesnoodles1984
text:
First, Forged in Fire never tasks the blacksmiths to replicate the weapons.  They ask the blacksmiths to forge a weapon of the same type.  Second, in the past, the show had been pretty good about holding blacksmiths to specifications that define the weapon.  For example, if a weapon was known to have a specific style of hilt, the blacksmith would be disqualified for putting a different style of hilt on it.  Basically, a little slack needs to be given.
That being said, the commentary in the nit picking is hilarious.  It works for both of you.  Keep it coming.

author: @matthewbulmer533
text: My uncle was in a blacksmithing group in my region, the group was approached by the show runners looking for people to be on it. No one was willing, NONE. It's a reality show, you know where it's all real except for everything you see. I've learned a small amount doing smithing with my uncle and I have seen things from clips where even I have to go "ahh no, you can't do that in that time frame" Just getting steel to proper working temps isn't doable with some of the things these "contestants" try to do.

author: @user-vm3os4wl1d

Saw a previous video, you were viewing a youtube vid, you go back and forth, trying to find the right moment in video, thought this might help.

(.)	While the video is paused, skip to the next frame.
(,)	While the video is paused, go back to the previous frame.

text: I've noticed that the lighter, refined blade usually wins, unless it fails. So refinement matters.  You guys would be surprised at how often the light and refined blade will decapitate far better than the heavy one.

replies.author: @PeregrinPursuivant
replies.text: The problem is that with a small sword, you want a stiff blade

replies.author: @mago8385
replies.text: ​@PeregrinPursuivant with a small sword you wand a edgeless more rigid blade because it's not meant to cut but stab

replies.author: @PeregrinPursuivant
replies.text: ​@mago8385 small swords are stiffer so that they can parry more. That's why some have a triangular blade, so that they are stiffer. Stiffness also helps with piercing, as less energy is lost in bending the sword. That's why anti mail swords are often super stift as well.
